title: Chapter 1: Basic Node And Express - Quiz
description: Test your understanding of Node.js basics, Express setup, routing, middleware, and request handling

questions.question: In Express, which method is used to start a server and listen for incoming connections on a specific port?
questions.explanation: The app.listen(port) method binds and listens for connections on the specified host and port. It is the standard way to start an Express server.

questions.question: What does the express.static() middleware do?

options.text: It prevents files from being modified on the server
It caches all responses in memory
It serves static files such as HTML, CSS, images, and JavaScript from a specified directory
It compresses all static content before serving

questions.explanation: express.static() is a built-in middleware function that serves static files (HTML, CSS, images, JS) from a directory you specify. For example, app.use(express.static('public')) serves files from the 'public' folder.

questions.question: How do you define a route parameter in an Express route path?
questions.explanation: Express uses the colon syntax to define route parameters. For example, '/users/:id' captures the value in req.params.id. This is a core feature of Express routing.

questions.question: Which object property in Express contains the parsed query string parameters from a URL like /search?term=hello&limit=10?
questions.explanation: req.query contains the parsed query string parameters. For /search?term=hello&limit=10, req.query would be { term: 'hello', limit: '10' }. req.params holds route parameters, and req.body holds POST body data.

questions.question: What does calling next() inside an Express middleware function do?

options.text: It sends the response back to the client
It restarts the request from the beginning
It passes control to the next middleware function in the stack
It skips to the last middleware in the chain

questions.explanation: next() passes control to the next middleware function in the Express middleware stack. If next() is not called and no response is sent, the request will hang. This is fundamental to how Express middleware chaining works.

questions.question: Which middleware is needed to parse JSON request bodies in Express 4.16+?

options.text: JSON parsing is automatic and requires no middleware

questions.explanation: Since Express 4.16, express.json() is a built-in middleware that parses incoming requests with JSON payloads and populates req.body. Before 4.16, the separate body-parser package was required.

questions.question: What is the correct way to send a JSON response in Express?
questions.explanation: res.json(data) automatically sets the Content-Type header to application/json and sends the JSON-stringified data. While res.send() can also send JSON, res.json() is the explicit and preferred method.

questions.question: What does app.use() do when called without a path argument, like app.use(myMiddleware)?

options.text: It applies the middleware only to the root route '/'
It applies the middleware to every incoming request regardless of path
It throws an error because a path is required
It applies the middleware only to GET requests

questions.explanation: When app.use() is called without a path, it defaults to '/' which matches all routes. This means the middleware will execute for every incoming request, making it ideal for logging, authentication, and other cross-cutting concerns.

questions.question: Which HTTP method does app.get() handle in Express?

options.text: It handles GET and HEAD requests
It retrieves configuration values from the app

questions.explanation: app.get() routes HTTP GET requests to the specified path with the specified callback functions. For other HTTP methods, Express provides app.post(), app.put(), app.delete(), etc. Note: app.get() with a single string argument retrieves app settings, but with a path and callback it defines a route.

questions.question: How do you chain multiple middleware functions for a single route in Express?

options.text: app.get('/path', [middleware1, middleware2], handler) or app.get('/path', middleware1, middleware2, handler)

questions.explanation: Express allows chaining middleware by passing multiple callback functions as arguments to a route method, either as separate arguments or as an array. Each middleware must call next() to pass control to the next function in the chain.
